item.title: HelloWorld
item.description:
Overview

This is the earliest public repo in my GitHub history. It is extremely small, but it marks the point where I started using GitHub as a place to keep even the simplest experiments.

What It Represents

  • first public repo setup
  • basic GitHub workflow practice
  • a baseline before the later course and app projects became more structured

Stack

  • repository scaffold

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/HelloWorld

item.title: 359PJ
item.description:
Overview

359PJ looks like the initial repository for a bookmarking-themed course project. The repo itself is minimal, but it appears to be the starting point for a project line that later turned into multiple BookMarkers repos.

What It Represents

  • initial project setup
  • early iteration before implementation stabilized
  • first pass at structuring the bookmarking project work

Stack

  • repo scaffold

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/359PJ

item.title: 359BookMarkers
item.description:
Overview

359BookMarkers is an early Java implementation of the bookmarking project thread. The repo contains a Java source tree plus compiled output, which suggests it was one of the first working versions rather than just a planning repo.

What It Represents

  • moving from scaffold to implementation
  • early Java application structure
  • a concrete iteration of the bookmarking idea

Stack

  • Java

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/359BookMarkers

item.title: 359 Final ABS-ABS
item.description:
Overview

This repo looks like another course-project branch in Java, likely focused on final-project packaging and class structure experimentation. The repo is small, but it sits in the same period as the other 359 project work and seems to represent a parallel or intermediate implementation line.

What It Represents

  • final-project iteration work
  • Java code organization around a course deliverable
  • experimentation before the later, more complete 359FFFF repo

Stack

  • Java

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/359_Final_ABS-ABS-

item.title: 359 PJ BookMarks
item.description:
Overview

359_PJ_BookMarks appears to be a later packaging repo in the same bookmarking course-project family. The repository is almost empty, which usually means it was used as a handoff, reset point, or cleanup branch rather than the main development home.

What It Represents

  • late-stage repo organization
  • project handoff or reset point
  • another step in the BookMarkers iteration history

Stack

  • repo scaffold

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/359_PJ_BookMarks

item.title: 359FFFF
item.description:
Overview

359FFFF looks like the most complete version of the 359 project line. The repo includes Java code, UI assets, UML files, and both SQLite and MySQL drivers, which strongly suggests this was the polished desktop-app variant of the BookMarkers work.

What It Represents

  • the most production-like 359 repo
  • Java UI work plus local database integration
  • a version where structure, assets, and dependencies were all brought together

Stack

  • Java
  • HTML/CSS
  • SQLite
  • MySQL connector
  • JFoenix UI library

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/359FFFF

item.title: Ruby
item.description:
Overview

This repo is extremely small, but it marks the start of a Ruby learning phase. It looks like a scratch repo used to anchor notes or simple exercises while I was switching into Rails-oriented work.

What It Represents

  • language ramp-up
  • lightweight experimentation
  • transition into Ruby and Rails projects

Stack

  • Ruby

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/Ruby

item.title: hello_app
item.description:
Overview

hello_app is a classic Ruby on Rails learning app. The repo contains the standard Rails structure with app, bin, config, Gemfile, and deployment-related files, so it reads like a hands-on tutorial or first MVC project rather than a toy single-file experiment.

What It Represents

  • first real Rails app structure
  • learning MVC conventions in practice
  • moving from language basics into web app delivery

Stack

  • Ruby
  • Ruby on Rails
  • HTML/CSS/JavaScript

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/hello_app

item.title: 420repo
item.description:
Overview

420repo appears to be another Rails-era repo living next to the hello_app work. The structure points to the same technology stack, which suggests it was either a parallel practice repo or a separate checkpoint during the same learning cycle.

What It Represents

  • Rails workflow repetition
  • learning by rebuilding similar shapes more than once
  • a separate repo used to reinforce the same stack

Stack

  • Ruby
  • Ruby on Rails
  • HTML/CSS/JavaScript

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/420repo

item.title: sleepTest
item.description:
Overview

sleepTest is another small Rails-period repo. The top-level structure again points to a Ruby app workflow, so I treat it as part of the same phase where I was learning by spinning up multiple small applications and testing the full stack repeatedly.

What It Represents

  • short-cycle experimentation
  • repeated Rails setup and app structure practice
  • using separate repos to isolate small ideas

Stack

  • Ruby
  • Ruby on Rails
  • HTML/CSS/JavaScript

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/sleepTest

item.title: MyBlog
item.description:
Overview

MyBlog was one of my early attempts at owning a personal publishing surface. The repo description explicitly calls it a personal blog built with React, and the top-level files show both frontend code and an LNMP deployment bundle, which makes it more operational than a simple UI demo.

What It Represents

  • an early personal-site build
  • React as the presentation layer
  • hands-on deployment thinking instead of frontend-only work

Stack

  • React
  • JavaScript
  • HTML/CSS
  • LNMP deployment tooling

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/MyBlog

item.title: yutongjin.github.io
item.description:
Overview

This repo looks like a GitHub Pages deployment target for an earlier personal site. The file tree is static-site shaped, with generated pages, assets, categories, and archives already materialized rather than source templates alone.

What It Represents

  • a GitHub Pages-based personal site
  • static publishing before the current Hugo setup
  • a stage where content and generated output lived together

Stack

  • HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript
  • GitHub Pages

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/yutongjin.github.io

item.title: LOLSummonerInfoApp
item.description:
Overview

LOLSummonerInfoApp is a React app built around League of Legends player information. The repo structure is straight Create React App, which suggests the focus was on product behavior and API/UI integration rather than custom build tooling.

What It Represents

  • game-adjacent product building
  • React single-page app workflow
  • pulling structured data into a user-facing dashboard

Stack

  • React
  • JavaScript
  • HTML/CSS
  • Create React App

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/LOLSummonerInfoApp

item.title: NorthEast
item.description:
Overview

NorthEast is a Java/Maven project with ANTLR-generated code plus frontend assets. Even with a sparse README, the structure suggests a project that combined language tooling or parsing work with a browser-facing layer, which makes it different from the earlier CRUD-style app repos.

What It Represents

  • a more technical Java project shape
  • parser or language-processing work through ANTLR
  • combining backend logic with a user-facing interface

Stack

  • Java
  • Maven
  • ANTLR
  • HTML/CSS/JavaScript

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/NorthEast

item.title: votwin
item.description:
Overview

votwin is a lightweight idea repo described as “vote and win.” There is not much code or documentation, but it reads like a product concept capture rather than a random scratchpad.

What It Represents

  • a simple product prototype idea
  • packaging a concept into a named repo early
  • using GitHub as a place to hold small experiments, not only full builds

Stack

  • concept repo

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/votwin

item.title: yutong_blog
item.description:
Overview

yutong_blog looks like a source-side or alternate-branch version of an earlier static personal site. The repo contains generated pages and assets in a way that suggests a full site export rather than a component-only frontend project.

What It Represents

  • another iteration of the personal site line
  • static content publishing before the current Hugo workflow
  • a transition point between older site generations and the current blog

Stack

  • HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/yutong_blog

item.title: aram-beimei
item.description:
Overview

aram-beimei is a Django app with Docker packaging and Fly.io deployment notes. This is one of the clearest recent repos because the README includes actual run, migration, and deploy commands, which makes the intended workflow explicit.

What It Represents

  • a more operational Python web app
  • Django plus container-based deployment
  • a repo where local development and production deployment were both documented

Stack

  • Python
  • Django
  • Docker
  • Fly.io
  • HTML templates

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/aram-beimei

item.title: blog
item.description:
Overview

This is the current blog source repo: Hugo content, theme customizations, GitHub Actions deployment, and the private iPhone publishing flow I built later. Compared with the older blog repos, this version is much more intentional about content structure and deployment boundaries.

What It Represents

  • the current writing system
  • cleaner separation between source, build, and deploy
  • custom tooling for posting from mobile without relying on the GitHub UI

Stack

  • Hugo
  • PaperMod
  • GitHub Actions
  • GitHub Pages
  • Python publisher service
  • Tailscale Funnel

Repo

  • GitHub: yutongjin/blog

item.title: Design Doc: Posting to My Hugo Blog Directly from iPhone
item.description:
Design Doc: Posting to My Hugo Blog Directly from iPhone

1. Problem

I wanted a low-friction way to publish to my blog from my iPhone.

The original blog setup was simple:

  • content lives in Markdown inside a Hugo repo
  • GitHub Actions builds and deploys the site
  • GitHub Pages serves the final static output

That setup is good for reliability, but not good for writing on mobile. Editing Markdown through GitHub’s mobile UI technically works, but it is still a repo workflow, not a writing workflow.

item.title: love tian
item.description: 我爱恬

item.title: Write
item.description:
Update the blog from your iPhone.

Preferred flow:

  • Open Admin to create a post in the private mobile editor.

Fallback flow:

  • Use GitHub’s mobile editor if the private publisher is unavailable.

Best flow:

  1. Install the GitHub app on your iPhone and sign in.
  2. Open one of the links below from Safari.
  3. Make the edit, commit to main, and GitHub Actions will publish the site.

Quick links:

  • New post
  • Edit Now page
  • Edit Projects page
  • All posts folder

Notes:

item.title: Relational DB vs NoSQL
item.description:
Relational DB vs NoSQL — Interview Answer Template (with QPS Details)

1. Throughput & Scalability

A relational database like MySQL can handle moderate QPS well.

Rough Numbers (rule of thumb, varies by setup):

  • Single MySQL instance
    • ~1K – 5K QPS (typical production range)
  • With read replicas
    • Reads can scale to 10K+ QPS
  • With sharding
    • Can scale to 100K+ QPS, depending on number of shards

However, sharding introduces additional complexity:

  • Choosing a good shard key
  • Avoiding hotspot partitions
  • Handling cross-shard queries
  • Rebalancing data

Example Tradeoffs:

  • Shard by userId

item.title: Put down your feelings
item.description:
🧠 Rebuilding Trust in Effort — A Practical Framework

🎯 Core Insight

You’re not someone who lacks discipline or ability.

You lost trust in effort.

  • From some of your past experiences, your brain learned:
    “Effort does not guarantee results”

So later, you subconsciously held back to avoid disappointment.

⚠️ The Real Problem

It’s not:

  • Laziness
  • Lack of ambition

It is:

A broken feedback loop between effort and reward

🔁 What You Need to Rebuild

You need to re-establish this loop:

item.title: Practice 4-10
item.description:
Lessons Learned from My Product Discovery Journey

Key Lessons

1. Start with a minimal product scope

I learned that I should begin with a small and clear set of functional requirements instead of adding extra features too early. In system design interviews, simple is usually better than broad. I should focus first on the core user needs and avoid introducing features that are not explicitly required.

2. Always call out non-functional requirements early

One of my biggest takeaways is that I need to explicitly mention non-functional requirements such as:

item.title: Change Data Capture (CDC) Summary
item.description:
Change Data Capture (CDC) Summary

  • Purpose: Track and propagate data changes (insert, update, delete) from a source to downstream systems.

  • Write-Ahead Log (WAL): In relational databases (e.g., PostgreSQL), all changes are logged before being applied. CDC reads from the WAL to capture changes.

  • NoSQL: In systems like DynamoDB, item-level streams capture changes in a similar fashion.

  • Pipeline Actions:

    • Detect changes.
    • Send to downstream systems.
    • Examples: replicate to read replicas, sync to a search index (Elasticsearch), or forward to Kafka.

  • Key Considerations:

item.title: Practice 4-9
item.description:
Learning

🎬 Video Upload & Moderation System (TikTok-style)

1. Functional Requirements

  • Creators can upload videos
  • Users can view videos
  • Reviewers can moderate videos
  • Users get notified if content is banned

2. Non-Functional Requirements

  • High availability (eventual consistency acceptable)
  • High throughput for uploads and reads
  • Low latency for video playback
  • Scalable for viral (hot) content

3. High-Level Architecture

Upload Flow

Client → API Gateway → UploadService → S3
↓
Kafka

Read Flow

User → CDN → S3

item.title: 10 tips for SD
item.description:
10 System Design Tips (Senior-Level)

April 8, 2026
Yutong Jin

🧠 System Design Key Principles

1. Start with Functional Requirements

Clearly define what the system needs to do:

  • Core APIs (read/write flows)
  • User interactions
  • Edge cases

💡 Tip: Do NOT spend too long here — 2–3 minutes is enough in interviews.

2. Identify the Core Bottleneck Early

Before designing, ask:

  • What is the expected QPS?
  • Where will the system break first?

Common bottlenecks:

item.title: System Design Interview Feedback (Calendar System)
item.description:
System Design Interview Feedback (Calendar System)

🟢 Overall Feedback

  • Functional design was mostly correct
  • Reminder workflow was well structured
  • High-level direction was solid
  • Some gaps existed but were mostly trivial

🧩 Requirements & Scoping

✅ Strengths

  • Covered core functional requirements
  • API design was reasonable
  • Non-functional requirements were acceptable

⚠️ Improvements

  • Did not explicitly capture recurring events early
    • Impacts storage, reads, and notifications

🧱 Data Modeling & Schema

✅ Strengths

  • Entity design was reasonable
  • Core fields identified correctly

⚠️ Improvements

  • Avoid defining full schema too early
  • Do schema after high-level design
  • Partitioning strategy needs improvement

🧠 Partitioning

❌ Current approach

  • Partition by event ID

⚠️ Problem

  • Optimizes writes, not reads

✅ Recommended

  • Partition by time (start time / next start time)

💡 Reason

  • Main read paths:
    • GET events by date range
    • Scheduler scans
  • Time-based partitioning narrows scan range and improves performance

🔁 Recurring Events Design

❌ Problematic approach

  • Pre-populating all occurrences

Issues

  • Data duplication
  • Infinite recurrence problems
  • Hard to update or cancel

✅ Recommended

  • Store recurrence rules and frequency
  • Return to client
  • Let client compute occurrences

💡 Benefits

  • Avoid duplication
  • Better scalability
  • Easier maintenance

🔔 Notification / Scheduler Design

✅ Strengths

  • Scheduler + queue architecture was correct
  • Recognized pattern: calendar = distributed scheduler

⭐ Strong point

  • Correct use of delayed queue (e.g., SQS)

🧠 System Pattern Recognition

User provides data now, system acts later → scheduler pattern

item.title: Designing Instagram: Media Upload & Feed System
item.description:
Overview

When designing an Instagram-like system, two of the most critical components are:

  • Media uploading pipeline
  • Feed generation strategy

This post summarizes key design decisions and tradeoffs.

1. Media Uploading Pipeline

High-level flow

  1. Client requests a pre-signed URL from backend
  2. Backend returns S3 pre-signed URL
  3. Client uploads media directly to S3 (chunked upload)
  4. S3 triggers an event (e.g., via notification / event bridge)
  5. Backend processes the upload asynchronously

Key design decisions

1. Create post record early

Before upload starts:

item.title: Layoff
item.description:
Both the first- and second-level leaders were laid off on the same day. The team suddenly lost its sense of direction, moving like a group of headless flies.

The external market offers little comfort. Every round of layoffs pushes another wave of strong candidates into the job pool. Hundreds of people compete for a single role, and even getting a call from HR now feels like a stroke of luck.

item.title: Current me 111
item.description:
• 👨‍💻 Software Engineer
  • 📚 最近在研究：Distributed Systems
  • ✍️ 正在写：瞎写

item.title: Hello World
item.description:
hi 这是我重新开始写博客的第一篇文章。

过去几年，我主要把精力放在 工作、系统设计和工程实践 上，很少系统性地记录自己的思考。但随着经验的积累，我越来越意识到：

不写下来，很多东西会慢慢消失。

为什么重新写博客？

主要有三个原因：

1. 把零散经验变成长期资产

在工程实践中，我接触了不少有挑战的问题，比如：

  • 高并发系统设计
  • 数据一致性与可用性权衡
  • 后端系统如何和 ML pipeline 协作
  • 真实生产环境中的 trade-offs

这些东西如果只存在于脑子里，价值会迅速衰减；
写下来，反而会不断“复利”。

2. 逼自己把问题想清楚

很多时候：

  • “我感觉我懂了” ≠ “我真的能讲清楚”

写博客是一个很残酷但有效的过程，它会暴露：

  • 模糊的概念
  • 没想清楚的边界
  • 似是而非的结论

这是我非常看重的一点。

3. 给未来的自己留一条线索

这不是一个“教程博客”，
更多是给 未来的自己 留的备忘录。

如果这些内容刚好也能帮到别人，那是额外的惊喜。

接下来会写什么？

接下来我大概率会写这些主题：

  • 🧠 系统设计 & 分布式系统
  • ⚙️ 后端工程实践（真实踩坑）
  • 🤖 ML Infra / MLOps
  • 📈 技术成长 & 职业思考
  • ✍️ 一些不太成熟但真实的想法

节奏不会快，但会尽量 长期更新。

如果你看到这里，感谢阅读。
博客，正式重新启动。
